questionList.questionTitle: B force on charges - negative ion enters B field
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A negatively charged particle is moving toward the top of the page when it enters a region of space with a uniform magnetic field B→ directed to the right, as shown in the figure. In what direction does the magnetic field exert a force on the particle immediately after the particle enters the region?

optionList.optionContent: To the right
To the left
Out of the page
Into the page

questionOption:
A .

To the right

B .

To the left

C .

Out of the page

D .

Into the page

questionList.questionTitle: Dielectrics - how do capacitance and field change
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates is charged and then disconnected from the source of emf. If the space between the plates is now filled with a dielectric material, what happens to the capacitance of the capacitor and the magnitude of the electric field between the plates?

CapacitanceMagnitude of the Electric Field
AIncreasesIncreases
BIncreasesDecreases
CDecreasesIncreases
DDecreasesDecreases

optionList.optionContent: A
B
C
D

questionOption:
A .

A

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.questionTitle: E field and isoline map - where does electron go
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: An electric field E→ within a region of space is represented by the arrows shown in the figure. The dashed-line curves represent equipotential lines for three electric potentials, VA, VB, and VC. An electron is released from rest at Point P on the VB equipotential. Which of the following correctly indicates the resulting motion of the electron and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons move toward stronger electric fields.
The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons experience an electric force in the direction of the electric field.
The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons move toward lower electric potentials.
The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons always accelerate toward higher potential.

questionOption:
A .

The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons move toward stronger electric fields.

B .

The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons experience an electric force in the direction of the electric field.

C .

The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons move toward lower electric potentials.

D .

The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons always accelerate toward higher potential.

questionList.questionTitle: Power - energy dissipated when I equals alpha t
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The current in a resistor of resistance R0 varies with time t. Starting at t=0, the current in the resistor is I(t)=αt, where α is a positive constant. How much energy is dissipated by the resistor during the time interval 0≤t≤tf ?

optionList.optionContent: αR0tf
α2R0tf2
2α2R0tf
13α2R0tf3

questionOption:
A .

αR0tf

B .

α2R0tf2

C .

2α2R0tf

D .

13α2R0tf3

questionList.questionTitle: EM induction - emf for square loop in xy-plane
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A square conducting loop of wire is initially in the xy-plane with its center located at the origin. There is a magnetic field that is uniform in space, but not necessarily constant in time, that is initially described by B→=B0(i^+k^) at time t=0. Which of the following would cause an emf to be induced in the loop of wire?

optionList.optionContent: Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^cos(ωt)+k^) while keeping the loop stationary.
Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^+k^cos(ωt)) while keeping the loop stationary.
Rotating the loop about the z-axis while keeping the field constant in time.
Moving the loop in the positive z-direction while keeping the field constant in time.

questionOption:
A .

Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^cos(ωt)+k^) while keeping the loop stationary.

B .

Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^+k^cos(ωt)) while keeping the loop stationary.

C .

Rotating the loop about the z-axis while keeping the field constant in time.

D .

Moving the loop in the positive z-direction while keeping the field constant in time.

questionList.questionTitle: Magnetic material - iron in field of electromagnet
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A ferromagnetic sample of metal is held in a strong external magnetic field created by a nearby magnet for a long time. The magnet is then moved far away from the sample. Which of the following correctly describes the magnetization of the sample after the nearby magnet is removed, and explains why?

optionList.optionContent: The sample is magnetized. The magnetic domains of the sample will become aligned with the external field and will remain in that alignment after the sample is removed from the external field.
The sample is magnetized. Free electrons in the sample will become polarized in the presence of the external field and will remain polarized after the sample is removed from the external field.
The sample is unmagnetized. The sample will have an equal number of magnetic dipoles aligned with the north and south poles of the nearby magnet.

The sample is unmagnetized. After becoming aligned when the magnet was nearby, the atoms’ magnetic dipoles in the sample return to being randomly oriented when the external field is no longer present.

questionOption:
A .

The sample is magnetized. The magnetic domains of the sample will become aligned with the external field and will remain in that alignment after the sample is removed from the external field.

B .

The sample is magnetized. Free electrons in the sample will become polarized in the presence of the external field and will remain polarized after the sample is removed from the external field.

C .

The sample is unmagnetized. The sample will have an equal number of magnetic dipoles aligned with the north and south poles of the nearby magnet.

D .

The sample is unmagnetized. After becoming aligned when the magnet was nearby, the atoms’ magnetic dipoles in the sample return to being randomly oriented when the external field is no longer present.

questionList.questionTitle: Conductors - hollow shell and point charge plus-q
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A positive point charge +q is located to the left of a hollow conducting shell, as shown in the figure. There is zero net charge on the shell. Which of the following indicates the direction, if any, of the electric field at Point P at the center of the conducting shell and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: The field is zero and has no direction. The electric potential is the same everywhere inside of a conducting shell.
The field is zero and has no direction. At the center of a closed, conducting shell, the field created by uniform charge distributions on the shell’s inner and outer surfaces must be zero.
The field direction is toward the right. In the nonconducting region inside a conducting shell, the field is that of any external charge or charge distribution.

The field direction is toward the right. Within a conducting shell, charge distributed on the surface of the shell reduces, but does not cancel, the field from any external charge or charge distribution.

questionOption:
A .

The field is zero and has no direction. The electric potential is the same everywhere inside of a conducting shell.

B .

The field is zero and has no direction. At the center of a closed, conducting shell, the field created by uniform charge distributions on the shell’s inner and outer surfaces must be zero.

C .

The field direction is toward the right. In the nonconducting region inside a conducting shell, the field is that of any external charge or charge distribution.

D .

The field direction is toward the right. Within a conducting shell, charge distributed on the surface of the shell reduces, but does not cancel, the field from any external charge or charge distribution.

questionList.questionTitle: E field of distribution - semicirc - point charge
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Point C is located at the center of a circle, which is represented by the dashed line in the figure. Along the top half of the circle is a thin, semicircular ring made of an insulating material containing a uniformly distributed positive charge +Q. A point charge of the same total charge +Q is located at the bottommost point on the circle. Which of the following correctly indicated the direction, if any, of the electric field E→C at Point C and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: E→C is zero and has no direction. The semicircle has equal amounts of charge in the left half and the right half.
E→C is zero and has no direction. There are equal amounts of charge above and below Point C, all at the same distance from Point C.
E→C is directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field due to the ring is zero at Point C, so only the point charge contributes to the field.
E→C is nonzero and directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field at Point C due to the ring is nonzero but less than that due to the point charge.

questionOption:
A .

E→C is zero and has no direction. The semicircle has equal amounts of charge in the left half and the right half.

B .

E→C is zero and has no direction. There are equal amounts of charge above and below Point C, all at the same distance from Point C.

C .

E→C is directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field due to the ring is zero at Point C, so only the point charge contributes to the field.

D .

E→C is nonzero and directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field at Point C due to the ring is nonzero but less than that due to the point charge.

26m/s

questionList.questionTitle: Inductance - emf when current is alpha t-squared
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: An inductor in a circuit has an inductance L and the current I in the inductor varies with time t according to the equation I(t)=αt2, where α is a constant. The induced emf of the inductor is zero at time t=0. What is the magnitude of the induced emf of the inductor as a function of time?

optionList.optionContent: 2αt
2Lαt
13αt3
13Lαt3

questionOption:
A .

2αt

B .

2Lαt

C .

13αt3

D .

13Lαt3

questionList.questionTitle: B force on charges - charged block in B field

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


A nonconducting block is sliding to the right across a rough horizontal surface within a region that contains a uniform magnetic field of magnitude B that is directed out of the page. The nonconducting block has mass M and has a net positive charge +Q distributed uniformly throughout its volume. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface is μ, and at time t=0 the velocity of the block is v0 in the +x-direction, as shown in Figure 1.

(a)

  • i. On the dot in Figure 2, which represents the block at time t=0, draw and label the forces (not components) exerted on the block. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the dot.

• ii. Derive, but do NOT solve, a differential equation for the block’s velocity v as a function of time t. Express your answer in terms of v, t, M, Q, B, μ, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

• iii. Using the differential equation from part (a)(ii), derive an expression for v as a function of t. Express your answer in terms of the same quantities listed in part (a)(ii), as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

The block is dropped from a height above the magnetic field so that it is moving downward with speed v0 when it first enters the magnetic field, as shown in Figure 3. A uniform electric field is added to the region of uniform magnetic field such that the block continues to move downward with a constant speed v0.

(b) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the electric field that will allow the block to continue moving downward with a constant speed v0. Express your answer in terms of M, Q, B, v0, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

questionList.questionTitle: RC circuits - Potential position and I-vs-t graphs

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

A circuit is constructed using a switch, a battery with emf Ɛ, two identical resistors of resistance R, and a capacitor of capacitance C, as shown in Figure 1. Points W, X, Y, and Z are indicated in the figure. The switch is initially open and the capacitor is initially uncharged. The following process then occurs:

  • At time t=0, the switch is closed.

  • At time t1, the circuit has reached steady state.

  • At time t2, the switch is reopened.

  • At time t3, the circuit has again reached steady state.

(a) Time tX is a short time after t=0, but before the circuit reaches steady state at time t1. The partially completed bar chart in Figure 2 shows that the electric potential at Point W is zero.

  • In Figure 2, draw a bar to represent the electric potential at points X, Y, and Z at time tX.
  • If the potential is zero at any of the points, write “Zero” in the corresponding column.

(b) Derive an expression for the charge Q on the positive plate of the capacitor as a function of time t for times after t2. Express your answer in terms of Ɛ, R, C, t, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) The current in the branch of the circuit containing the capacitor is I. On the axes in Figure 3, sketch a graph of I as a function of t. Take positive current to be clockwise.

The time interval between when the switch is reopened at t2 and the circuit reaches steady state at t3 is approximated by the value Δt23.

The circuit is then changed by replacing both resistors with new resistors of resistance 2R. The process of closing and later reopening the switch is repeated. The new time for the changed circuit to reach steady state after the switch is reopened is Δtnew.

(d) Determine the ratio ΔtnewΔt23. Briefly justify your reasoning.

questionList.questionTitle: EM induction - frequency of changing mag field

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


A conducting bar is on and perpendicular to a pair of parallel, horizontal conducting rails. The width w between the rails can be changed. The rails extend into a region where there is a uniform magnetic field of known magnitude B0 that is directed out of the page. The left ends of the rails extend into the magnetic field, as shown in the top view in Figure 1.

The experiment is set up so that the bar is pulled to the left at constant speed v0. The bar is always perpendicular to the rails as the bar moves to the left. The emf induced in a conducting bar moving with speed v perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of magnitude B is equal to BLv, where L is the length of the bar.

A group of students are asked to create a linear graph that can be used to experimentally calculate the constant speed v0 of the bar as it is pulled to the left. In addition to the equipment described, the students are only permitted to use a voltmeter and a meterstick.

(a) Describe an experimental procedure to collect data that would allow the students to create a linear graph that can be used to experimentally calculate the constant speed v0 of the bar as it is pulled to the left. Include any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty.
(b) Describe how the data could be graphed, and how that graph would be analyzed, to determine the constant speed v0 of the bar.

The bar and rails are removed from the magnetic field. Several coils of wire, each made of 25 loops of wire of different radii r, are placed in the magnetic field. The magnetic field is then made to vary sinusoidally with time t according to the equation B=B1sin(2πft), where B1 is the amplitude of the field and f=100Hz. The maximum induced emf Ɛmax is measured for each coil.

(c) A group of students is asked to experimentally determine the field amplitude B1 using the coils of wire. The data recorded by the students is shown in the table.

r(m)Ɛmax(V)
0.010.18
0.020.55
0.031.22
0.042.37
0.053.9

  • i. Indicate two quantities that could be graphed to produce a straight line that could be used to calculate an experimental value for B1. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Vertical axis: _____________          Horizontal axis: ____________
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the following graph. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units.
• iii. Draw the best-fit line for the data graphed in part (c)(ii).
(d) Using the straight line from part (c)(iii), calculate the value of B1.

The magnetic field is then made to vary sinusoidally with time t according to the equation B=B1sin(2πft), where B1 is the amplitude of the field and f=100Hz. (c) A group of students is asked to experimentally determine the field amplitude B1 using the coils of wire. The data recorded by the students is shown in the table.

r(m)Ɛmax(V)
0.010.18
0.020.55
0.031.22
0.042.37
0.053.9

  • i. Indicate two quantities that could be graphed to produce a straight line that could be used to calculate an experimental value for B1. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Vertical axis: _____________          Horizontal axis: ____________
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the following graph. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units.
• iii. Draw the best-fit line for the data graphed in part (c)(ii).
(d) Using the straight line from part (c)(iii), calculate the value of B1.

questionList.questionTitle: Distributed charge - uniform charge density sphere

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


A nonconducting sphere of radius R is centered on the origin. The sphere has a negative uniform volume charge density ρsphere=−ρ0. The sphere is surrounded by a nonconducting spherical shell of inner radius R and outer radius 2R. The shell has a positive uniform volume charge density ρshell=+ρ0, as shown in the figure. Points A, B, and C are located on the x-axis at positions x=0, x=2R, and x=3R, respectively.

The magnitude of the electric fields at points A, B, and C are EA, EB, and EC respectively.

(a) Rank the values EA, EB, and EC from greatest to least. Without using equations, justify your answer using physics principles.
(b) Derive an expression for EB in terms of R, ρ0, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Consider a new case where instead of having a uniform volume charge density, the charge density of the sphere varies with the radius of the sphere as given by ρsphere(r)=−ρ0Rr. In this case, the electric field at Point B is EB,new.

(c) Will the value of EB,new be greater than, less than, or equal to the value of EB, which was derived in part (b)? Justify your claim with relevant physics principles.

partList.directionsText:
PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM
SECTION I
Time—45 minutes
35 Questions
Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETTSM
SECTION II
Time—45 minutes
3 Questions
Directions: Answer all three questions. The suggested time is sbout 15 minutes for answering each of the questions, which are worth 15 points each. The parts within a question may not have equal weight. Show all your work in this booklet in the spaces provided after each part.
